community_note: Livestock and wildlife beginning seasonal migration earlier than the normal calendar — a strong signal that drought onset is imminent and pasture will fail sooner than expected

indicator: Animal Weakness and Body Condition Decline
community_note: Cattle losing weight, ribs becoming visible, reduced energy — indicates pasture quality and water availability are already below adequate levels
Elders monitor specific behavioural cues in cattle — restlessness, altered grazing direction — to estimate when the rainy season is about to begin
Elders observe cloud types, direction of movement, density, and colour to distinguish rain-bearing clouds from those associated with prolonged dry conditions

indicator: Sky Appearance at Dawn and Dusk
community_note: Colour, clarity, and overall appearance of the sky at sunrise and sunset are interpreted by elders as indicators of coming rain or continued drought
Unusually strong winds with low humidity from specific directions — identified by elders as a signal of delayed rainfall or an incoming dry period

indicator: Rate of Land and Ground Surface Drying
community_note: How quickly the topsoil and ground surface dry out between rainfall events — rapid drying relative to the season indicates soil moisture deficit and early drought stress
Abnormally high ambient temperatures and ground heat, assessed through community experience — combined with other indicators, signals drought onset
Early or accelerated drying and browning of seasonal grasses before the expected end of the rains — a primary trigger for livestock movement decisions

indicator: Unusual or Early Changes in Trees and Shrubs
community_note: Premature leaf drop, early flowering, or unusual changes in specific tree and shrub species that elders associate with coming dry conditions

Reading of star positions and celestial signs by specialist elders — an advanced tier of indigenous forecasting consulted when ordinary indicators are ambiguous. Distinct from general elder knowledge.
Forecasting by recognised divine seers in the most remote Samburu communities — the primary warning source for communities beyond the reach of formal EWS
